directions.name: Finish Flattening PTX Export Across Tile Rows
directions.hypothesis: Round 2/17 validated the PTX export-helper family: runtime improved from 26.978816 ms back down to 25.505328 ms, DRAM stayed anchored near 11.5%, and long-scoreboard dropped from 7.79 to 7.21. The current helper still recurses over `TileRow`, so the next best move is to finish that specialization and make the hot-band export fully explicit across the four tile rows. That keeps the single-stage scratch and recovered locality intact while trimming the remaining recursive control and repeated row-base setup in the epilogue.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX export-side control overhead and per-row epilogue orchestration inside the hot-band microkernel.
directions.risk: Low to medium. This continues the only family with a clean recent win and does not widen scope beyond the existing one-stage PTX export helper, but the remaining upside may now be incremental rather than step-sized.
directions.stop_condition: Stop extending the export-helper family if one more bounded flattening pass keeps DRAM stable but fails to beat 25.50532818 ms.

directions.name: Retune PTX Prefetch Handoff After The Export Cleanup
directions.hypothesis: With the export helper now partially flattened and locality preserved, the next alternate family remains the PTX `cp.async` handoff. A bounded retime of the wait/commit sequence or A/B staging order can test whether the remaining 7.21 long-scoreboard is now mostly feed latency rather than epilogue control.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Copy-pipeline handoff timing in the PTX hot-band steady-state loop.
directions.risk: Medium. This is still a live alternative family, but it changes the steady-state pipeline rather than the already-winning export surface, so the regression risk is higher than another helper cleanup pass.
directions.stop_condition: Stop after one bounded retime if the stall mix moves around without a runtime gain over the new accepted base.

directions.name: Tune PTX Hot-Band B-Shared Skew On Top Of The New Base
directions.hypothesis: The successful export cleanup kept DRAM flat while reducing scoreboard, so the remaining slack may now be in B-fragment addressing rather than traversal or export. A narrow PTX-only skew or address-generation cleanup around `kBSharedStride` and `b_shared_col_from_logical` can test whether there is still low-risk shared-layout headroom on top of the new accepted base.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory fragment address generation and B-tile layout friction in the PTX hot-band path.
directions.risk: Medium to high. This is a distinct family, but shared-layout edits can silently reintroduce the memory-side regression that the traversal experiment already exposed.
directions.stop_condition: Abandon this family if it raises DRAM or correctness risk without a measurable runtime improvement.

notes: Round 3/17 diagnosis for run 20260420_154827_bf16_gemm_v1_7adfc4e. Human-review mapping: continue to accept only narrow PTX-adjacent ideas on the recovered baseline; keep broad default-promotion, staged K32, paired export-lifetime, helper-flattening-without-locality-awareness, and x-major traversal closed. The key update is that the export-helper family is now confirmed live: the bounded row-pair specialization improved runtime to 25.50532818 ms while holding DRAM at 11.54 and reducing long-scoreboard to 7.21. That makes a final TileRow flattening pass the best next move, with PTX prefetch retiming and B-shared skew as the two backup families.
